children.name: Heliornis
children.description: A monotypic genus of the smallest and most colourful finfoot, distinguished by strikingly patterned feet and a slender, pointed bill. It is the only New World representative of the family.

children.name: Heliornis fulica
children.description: A small, secretive waterbird with brown upperparts, white underparts and a striking black-and-white striped neck, found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina. It has lobed feet like a grebe but a slender, rail-like body. Unique among birds, the male carries newly hatched chicks in pouches under his wings.

children.name: Podica
children.description: A large, robust finfoot with a long graduated tail and a distinctive white throat patch. It inhabits forested rivers and streams across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Podica senegalensis
children.description: The largest finfoot, reaching 60 centimetres in length, with brownish-black upperparts spotted with white and a long, heavy orange-red bill. It is a shy, crepuscular resident of densely vegetated rivers and streams across much of sub-Saharan Africa. It swims low in the water with only its head and neck visible, slipping into cover at the slightest disturbance.

children.name: Heliopais
children.description: A monotypic Asian genus of finfoot with a distinctive black mask and a thick neck. It is one of the most threatened waterbirds in Southeast Asia due to habitat loss.

children.name: Heliopais personatus
children.description: A rare and endangered waterbird with a black face mask, a heavy greenish-yellow bill and a white throat patch, found in Bangladesh, Southeast Asia and Sumatra. It frequents slow-moving rivers and mangroves with overhanging vegetation. With an estimated population of fewer than 1,000 individuals, it is one of Asia's most imperilled waterbirds.
